question: Why have I been referred to SCOTS?
answer: You have been referred to SCOTS to assess your musculoskeletal condition (e.g. concerns with your joints, muscles, or other soft tissues) and determine the most appropriate next step in your care. This may include advice and education, a structured rehabilitation plan, an education program, referral for imaging if needed, or referral for specialist orthopaedic opinion if appropriate.

question: What will happen at my appointment?
answer: We will listen to your concerns and goals, assess your condition, review any previous treatment, and discuss your diagnosis and options. Together, we will agree on a clear plan forward tailored to your specific situation.

question: Will I see a surgeon?
answer: Not at this stage. Your first appointment is with a Physiotherapist who specialises in assessing whether your condition is best managed without surgery, or whether you would benefit from a surgical consultation. This step helps ensure you receive the right care at the right time.

question: Why not go straight to a surgeon?
answer: For many conditions, surgery is not the first or best treatment. Symptoms often improve with the right non-surgical care, and seeing a surgeon too early may not change your outcome. Our role is to make sure that if you are referred for surgery, it is appropriate and likely to help you.

question: What if I think I need surgery?
answer: We understand that some patients are hoping for a surgical opinion. During your appointment we will explain whether surgery is likely to help, or whether you are likely to meet the threshold for surgical consideration in your situation. If appropriate, we will support a referral to Orthopaedics. If not, we will outline the best next steps and why. Surgery is not ruled out — it is carefully considered at the right time.

question: What if I have already tried physiotherapy?
answer: That is helpful information. We will look at what type of treatment you have had, how long you tried it for, and whether it was targeted and progressive. Sometimes a more structured approach can still lead to improvement.

question: What happens after the assessment?
answer: Depending on your assessment, we may start or adjust a rehabilitation plan, invite you to an education program, discharge you with advice, refer you for further investigations, or refer you for a surgical opinion. If a trial of treatment is recommended, we will set a clear timeframe and review your progress.

question: How can I prepare for my appointment?
answer: Bring any relevant scan reports or letters, wear comfortable clothing for assessment, and be ready to discuss how your condition affects your daily life. If you have any questions, please speak with your GP or the referring clinician.
